svg.text: MN 119 — KĀYAGATĀSATI SUTTA
Mindfulness of the Body
Mindfulness of the body, when cultivated and frequently practiced, is of great fruit and great benefit
kāyagatāsati bhāvitā bahulīkatā mahapphalā hoti mahānisaṁsā
BODY CONTEMPLATION PRACTICES · kāyagatāsati bhāvanā
Mindfulness while breathing in and out
ānāpānassati bhāvanā
Breathes mindfully; discerns long and short breaths;
trains to experience the whole body and settle bodily constructs.
Postures
iriyāpatha
Being aware of posture while walking, standing, sitting, or lying down.
In whatever way the body is oriented, one discerns it accordingly.
Acting with clear awareness
sampajānakārī
Acts with clear awareness in all activities:
when going forward, returning, looking, bending, eating, dressing,
drinking, defecating, urinating, speaking, and keeping silent.
Reviewing impurities in the body
kāye asuci paccavekkhana
Reviews the body from the soles upwards and
hair downwards as full of various impurities: skin, flesh,
sinews, bones, organs, stomach contents, fat, grease, and fluids.
Reviewing the elements in the body
kāye dhātu paccavekkhana
Reviews the body, however positioned,
in terms of the four elements: earth, water, fire, and wind —
like a butcher dividing a cow at a crossroads.
Nine Charnel Grounds
nava sivathikā
Compares the body to corpses in progressive decomposition, discerning:
‘This body too is of the same nature; it will become like that.’
Diligent, with continuous effort, resolute — worldly thoughts abandoned — mind internally steadied, calmed, unified, and collected
appamatta · ātāpī · pahitatta — gehasita sarasaṅkappā pahīyanti — ajjhattameva cittaṁ santiṭṭhati ekodi hoti samādhiyati
FOUR JHĀNAS · cattāro jhānā
First Jhāna· paṭhama jhāna
Accompanied by reflection (savitakka) · Examination (savicāra)
Born of seclusion (vivekaja) · Imbued with uplifting joy and pleasure (pītisukha)
kneading powder
Second Jhāna· dutiya jhāna
Internal tranquility (sampasādana) · Unification of mind (ekodibhāva)
Free from reflection and examination (avitakka · avicāra)
Born of collectedness (samādhija) · Imbued with uplifting joy and pleasure (pītisukha)
underground spring
Third Jhāna· tatiya jhāna
With the fading of desire for uplifting joy (pītiyā ca virāgā)
Equanimous (upekkhaka) · Mindful (sato) · Clearly aware (sampajāno)
Experiencing pleasure with the body (sukha kāyena)
submerged lotus
Fourth Jhāna· catuttha jhāna
Abandoning bodily pleasure and pain (sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā)
Settling of mental pleasure and displeasure (somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā)
Purification of mindfulness through equanimity (upekkhāsatipārisuddhi)
Experiencing a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling (adukkhamasukhaṁ)
covered from head to toe
in a spotless white cloth
UNCULTIVATED (abhāvitā) vs CULTIVATED (bhāvitā)
UNCULTIVATED
Māra finds an opening
Heavy rock thrown on wet clay — sinks in
(The unguarded mind is easily penetrated by distractions)
Dry sapless wood — fire lights easily
(Passions quickly ignite in a mind lacking awareness)
Empty water jar — fills readily
(A vacant mind easily absorbs unwholesome states)
CULTIVATED
Māra finds no opening
Light string ball on solid heartwood — bounces off
(Distractions cannot penetrate a solid, grounded mind)
Wet sappy wood — fire cannot light
(A mindful state is resistant to the flames of passion)
Full water jar — nothing enters
(A mind full of awareness has no room for unwholesome states)
The Skilled Charioteer
Like a charioteer driving wherever he wishes, he directs his cultivated mind to realize any phenomenon by direct knowledge
yenicchakaṁ yadicchakaṁ — abhiññāsacchikiriyāya cittaṁ abhininnāmeti
TEN BENEFITS · dasa ānisaṁsā

g.text: Patient endurance of physical hardship and pain
khamo hoti sītassa uṇhassa ... adhivāsakajātiko hoti
Effortless attainment of the four jhānas
Divine ear: hears divine and human sounds
Discerns the minds of other beings
Sees beings passing away and being reborn,
dibbena cakkhunā satte passati cavamāne upapajjamāne yathākammūpage

svg.text: Ending of Mental Defilements
āsavakkhaya
“ Through the wearing away of mental defilements, one realizes for oneself through direct knowledge,
the taintless liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, and having attained it, one abides in it ”
āsavānaṁ khayā anāsavaṁ cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharati
